content:
14 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every ServiceNow developer writes JavaScript.
But almost nobody stops to ask a simple question.
If ServiceNow is built on Java — and it is, entirely — why are we writing JavaScript? Where does our code actually run? And who, or what, is translating between the two?
The answer is a piece of software called Rhino. And once you understand what Rhino does, how it bridges two completely different programming worlds, something clicks. Every GlideRecord query, every Business Rule, every Script Include you’ve ever written suddenly makes a lot more sense.
Let’s pull back the curtain.

The Problem with Building Enterprise Software
Imagine you’re an architect at a company in 2003, tasked with building a platform that will eventually handle IT service management for thousands of enterprises. It needs to be fast, stable, concurrent, and secure. It needs to run 24/7 without crashing. It needs to handle hundreds of database transactions per second. It needs multi-tenancy, meaning thousands of companies share the same infrastructure without leaking data to each other.
You reach for Java. Not because Java is glamorous, but because Java is engineered for exactly this. The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) gives you thread management, garbage collection, connection pooling, and a battle-hardened concurrency model. Java has been deployed in banking systems and airline reservation systems. It’s verbose, it’s strict, and enterprises trust it with their most critical operations.
So ServiceNow is built on Java. The platform is essentially a large Java application, packaged and deployed on Apache Tomcat, the industry-standard Java web server.
But here’s the problem.
Java is powerful and Java is strict — almost to a fault. Writing customizations in raw Java would require ServiceNow customers to be Java developers, compile their code, package it, and deploy it through a full release pipeline just to change a simple business rule. That’s not a platform. That’s a software project.
What enterprises actually need is a way to configure and customize the system without touching the core. They need something that feels approachable. Something interpreted, flexible, and fast to iterate on.
They need a scripting language.

Enter Rhino: The Interpreter in the Middle
Rhino is a JavaScript engine written entirely in Java, originally developed by Netscape in the late 1990s and later open-sourced through the Mozilla project. Unlike V8 (which powers Node.js and Chrome) or SpiderMonkey, Rhino has a unique property: it runs inside a JVM. It doesn’t need a separate process. It doesn’t need a foreign function interface or inter-process communication. It lives natively inside a Java application.
Think of Rhino as a professional interpreter at a diplomatic summit. Two delegations — the Java platform and the JavaScript developer — need to communicate, but they speak completely different languages. The interpreter doesn’t change what either side is saying. He doesn’t add new meaning. He just translates, fluently and instantly, so both sides can work together as if the language barrier didn’t exist.
That’s exactly what Rhino does inside ServiceNow.
When you write a Business Rule in the ServiceNow script editor and click Save, your JavaScript isn’t sent to a Node.js server. It isn’t compiled into bytecode by a modern JIT compiler. It’s handed to Rhino, which interprets it at runtime inside the same JVM that’s running the entire ServiceNow platform. Rhino reads your code, executes it line by line, and whenever your script touches something like GlideRecord or gs.log(), Rhino reaches into the Java layer and calls the actual Java method underneath.
This is the architecture. JavaScript is the interface. Java is the engine. Rhino is the translator.

How Rhino Actually Works
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Let’s get into the specifics, because this is where it gets genuinely interesting.
Rhino parses JavaScript source code into an abstract syntax tree, then either interprets that tree directly or compiles it into Java bytecode before executing it. The bytecode path is faster and is typically used in production environments where the same script is executed many times.
But the real magic is in how Rhino exposes Java objects to JavaScript code.
Rhino has a feature called LiveConnect. LiveConnect allows JavaScript code running in Rhino to directly instantiate Java objects, call Java methods, and access Java fields as if they were native JavaScript objects. The two object models are different — JavaScript uses prototype-based inheritance, Java uses class-based inheritance — but Rhino handles the translation transparently.
Here’s the remarkable implication. When ServiceNow exposes GlideRecord to your script, it’s not implementing GlideRecord in JavaScript. There’s no JavaScript class called GlideRecord anywhere in the codebase. GlideRecord is a Java class. The Glide platform exposes it to Rhino, and Rhino makes it available in your JavaScript environment as if it were a native object.
This means every property access, every method call you make on a GlideRecord, crosses the Rhino bridge and executes real Java code on the other side.

What Actually Happens When You Write GlideRecord
Let’s trace a single query and follow it all the way through.
var gr = new GlideRecord('incident');
gr.addQuery('state', 1);
gr.query();
while (gr.next()) {
gs.log(gr.short_description);
}This looks like JavaScript. It reads like JavaScript. But what actually happens?
Step 1 — Rhino parses and executes your script. When ServiceNow triggers your Business Rule or Script Include, it hands the script text to Rhino. Rhino parses it and begins executing.
Step 2 — new GlideRecord('incident') crosses the bridge. Rhino sees that GlideRecord has been registered as a Java class in the scripting context. It calls the Java constructor for com.glide.script.GlideRecord (or similar internal class path), passing 'incident' as the argument. A real Java object is instantiated on the heap. Rhino wraps it in a JavaScript-compatible wrapper object and hands it back to your script as gr.
Step 3 — gr.addQuery('state', 1) calls a Java method. Your script accesses the addQuery method on the gr wrapper. Rhino resolves this to the Java method addQuery(String field, Object value) and invokes it. Inside that method, Java builds an internal query structure — not SQL yet, just an object model representing the conditions you've specified.
Step 4 — gr.query() fires the database call. This Java method takes the query object, generates the appropriate SQL for the underlying database (typically MySQL or MariaDB in older instances), checks ACLs, applies any active query conditions defined by the security model, and executes the SQL through a connection pool managed by the JVM. The results come back as a JDBC ResultSet.
Step 5 — gr.next() advances the cursor. Each call to next() moves through the ResultSet, mapping database column values to GlideRecord field wrappers. When your script reads gr.short_description, it isn't reading a plain string. It's calling a Java getter that returns a GlideElement object — another Java object wrapped by Rhino — which coerces to a string when you use it in a string context.
Step 6 — gs.log() routes to the platform logger. gs is an instance of GlideSystem, another Java object registered in the Rhino scripting context. Calling gs.log() invokes a Java logging method that writes to the platform's logging infrastructure.
Every single line of that five-line script was interpreted by Rhino, which delegated every meaningful operation to Java, which executed against a managed database connection. The JavaScript was the steering wheel. The Java was the engine doing all the actual work.

The Request Lifecycle: From Browser to Database
To understand the full picture, you need to zoom out even further.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When a user opens a ServiceNow form and submits an incident, here’s what happens:
Browser — The user clicks Submit. The browser sends an HTTP POST request to the ServiceNow instance URL.
Apache Tomcat — Tomcat is the Java web server that sits at the entry point. Think of Tomcat as the front desk receptionist of a large office building. Every incoming request is received here, assigned to a thread from the thread pool, and routed to the appropriate handler. Tomcat doesn’t process business logic. It just manages the flow of traffic in and out.
Java servlet layer — The request is handed to a Java servlet — essentially a Java class designed to handle web requests. This layer parses the HTTP request, authenticates the user session, validates CSRF tokens, and begins preparing the context for processing.
Transaction management — At this point, a database transaction is opened. Everything that happens from here until the response is sent either commits or rolls back as a unit. This is how ServiceNow maintains data integrity even when Business Rules, ACLs, and workflows are all firing in sequence.
Rhino engine activation — When the platform determines that scripts need to run — and they almost always do — a Rhino scripting context is initialized. The platform registers all the Glide APIs (GlideRecord, GlideSystem, GlideDateTime, etc.) into this context, making them available as JavaScript globals.
Business Rules execute — Before-insert, before-update, and after-update Business Rules run. Each one is a JavaScript script handed to Rhino. Rhino executes them sequentially, and each script has access to the full Glide API suite through the Rhino bridge.
Database operations — All the GlideRecord queries and updates accumulate through the Java layer into actual SQL statements, executed against the database through JDBC connection pools. Connection pools are important here: creating a new database connection is expensive. Pooling means connections are reused across requests, and the JVM manages which connections are available at any given time.
Response generation — The results come back up through the layers. The Java layer serializes the data into JSON or HTML, Tomcat sends the HTTP response back to the browser, and the user sees their confirmation screen.
The entire journey — browser to database and back — might take less than 300 milliseconds. That’s the architecture working exactly as designed.

Why Glide APIs Are Not JavaScript
This is a misconception worth addressing directly.
GlideRecord is not a JavaScript class. GlideSystem is not a JavaScript library. GlideDateTime is not a polyfill for JavaScript's built-in Date object.
All of these are Java classes, exposed to your JavaScript environment through Rhino. They behave like JavaScript objects because Rhino’s wrapper layer makes them behave that way. But they execute Java code. They use Java memory. They are subject to Java’s threading model.
This matters for practical reasons.
When you instantiate a GlideRecord inside a Business Rule, you’re creating a Java object on the JVM heap. When you call .query(), you're triggering Java code that manages database connections, respects query business rules, and enforces row-level security — all implemented in Java, all invisible to you at the JavaScript level.
When you call gs.nowDateTime(), you're calling a Java method that reads the server clock, applies timezone logic, and returns a formatted string. The intelligence is in Java. Your JavaScript just invokes it.
The Glide APIs are essentially a remote control for the Java platform. Your JavaScript is pressing buttons on the remote. The actual machinery — the motor, the logic, the hardware — is all Java underneath.

How This Architecture Scales to Fortune 500 Companies
Here’s where the engineering decisions pay off.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

ServiceNow runs on multi-tenant infrastructure, meaning thousands of companies share the same physical servers while their data remains completely
